source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 11.3.4-11.3.5 journald and journalctl (pp. 207-209)

questions.q: What kinds of log messages does journald capture?
options: Syslog messages, kernel messages, initramfs/startup logs, and the stdout/stderr of all services
questions.why: journald captures syslog, kernel, initramfs/startup logs and every service's standard output and error.

questions.q: In what format does journald store its logs?
options: A binary, indexed, structured format (read with journalctl)
questions.why: journald stores logs in an indexed binary format, which is why you need journalctl to read them.

questions.q: Does journald keep logs across reboots by default, and where are they stored?
options: No - by default it is not persistent; logs sit in /run/log/journal/ and are lost on reboot
No - logs are never stored anywhere
questions.why: Persistence is off by default; logs live in /run/log/journal/ and disappear when the system restarts.

questions.q: View the system journal (all captured log entries).
questions.why: journalctl with no options shows the log, similar to /var/log/messages but with priorities marked, local timestamps and rotated logs included.

questions.q: Follow the journal in real time (show the latest entries and keep displaying new ones).
questions.why: journalctl -f prints the last ten lines then streams new entries as they arrive.

questions.q: Show only journal entries with priority 'err' (error) or higher.
questions.why: journalctl -p <priority> filters by the given level and above; err can also be given as the number 3.

questions.q: In journalctl priorities, which numbers correspond to emerg and debug?
questions.why: Priorities run emerg(0), alert(1), crit(2), err(3), warning(4), notice(5), info(6), debug(7).
